== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.

{{Shrine | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{ill|36°06′57.9″N 133°06′32.2″E|ja|}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58年 *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

{{Shrine | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{ill|36°06′57.9″N 133°06′32.2″E|ja|}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58年 *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.

{{Shrine | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{ill|36°06′57.9″N 133°06′32.2″E|ja|}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.

'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{ill|36°06′57.9″N 133°06′32.2″E|ja|}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58年 *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.

The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.

Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>

{{Shrine | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{ill|36°06′57.9″N 133°06′32.2″E|ja|}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the modern ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.

Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9th year }} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} under the modern ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|12=draft}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉|12=draft}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9th year|12=draft}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社|12=draft}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=draft}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿|12=draft}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿|12=draft}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日|12=draft}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6
{{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{citation needed|Ujika Shrine}} <!--Ujika Shrine-->

It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿|12=draft}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿|12=draft}}.

14 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宇受賀命神社 up to rev 96851641
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 宇受賀命神社 |画像 = [[画像:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = 島根県隠岐郡海士町宇受賀747 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|06|57.9|N|133|06|32.2|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 宇受賀命 |社格 = 式内社（名神大）・旧郷社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = 隠岐造銅板葺 |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 7月11日 |神事 = }}
'''宇受賀命神社'''（うづかみことじんじゃ）は、[[島根県]][[隠岐郡]][[海士町]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。[[式内社]]（[[名神大社]]）で[[旧社格]]は[[郷社]]。[[島前]]の[[中ノ島 (島根県)|中ノ島]]の北端にあたる宇受賀部落の外れ、丘上に鎮座する。
== 祭神 == '''宇受賀命'''（うずかのみこと）を祀る。「宇須賀宮」や「宇津賀大明神」などと称され、一貫してこの地の[[守護神]]として祀られて来た他地に見えない当神社独自の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。神名は鎮座地「宇受賀」とも関係するものであろうが、語義不明。なお、[[元禄]]16年（[[1703年]]）の『島前村々神名記』には、祭神を「[[倉稲魂命|倉稲魂命（うかのみたまのみこと）]]」と記してあるが、これは「[[記紀]]」に載せる神名に付会させたものであろう<ref name="調査報告">『式内社調査報告』。</ref>。
== 由緒 == [[貞享]]5年（[[1688年]]）の『[[増補隠州記]]』によれば、[[嘉吉|嘉吉年中]]（15世紀中頃）に炎上して[[縁起]]を焼失したとあり、創祀の年代や事由は不明であるが上述のように鎮座地宇受賀の守護神として古くから祀られて来た。[[大正]]頃（20世紀前葉）の社蔵記録によると、[[遷座]]の時期は詳らかにしないものの、かつては現在地から北方150[[間]]（およそ270[[メートル]]）隔たった、海面から36、7[[尺]]程（およそ10メートル強）の高さの断崖上に鎮座していたという<ref name="調査報告"/>。『[[続日本後紀]]』に[[承和 (日本)|承和]]9年（[[842年]]）由良比女命神（[[由良比女神社]]）、水若酢命神（[[水若酢神社]]）とともに[[官社]]に預かったことが見え<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref>、隠岐国ではもっとも早く中央の史書に現れる神社の1社で、[[延喜式神名帳|延喜の制]]では[[延喜式神名帳|国幣大社]]（名神大社）に列した隠岐国4大社の1社でもあり、[[国内神名帳]]である『[[隠岐国神名帳]]』にも「従一位宇酒賀大明神」と記載を見る<ref>『続後紀』や『延喜式』での記載は「宇受'''加'''命神（社）」。なお、『国内神名帳』は玉若酢命神社の[[社家|神主家]]である[[億岐氏|億岐家]]に伝わるものであるが、後世の手が加わっているらしいことから、そこに見える「従一位」の[[神階]]は、[[吉田家]]の[[宗源宣旨]]によるもの、あるいは億岐氏の作為が働いたものと見られる（『式内社調査報告』）。</ref>。
中世を通じて[[国衙]]や[[守護|守護職]]から重きを置かれ、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]7年（[[1271年]]）に当地の[[田荘]]と[[目代]]から祭祀料として[[国衙領]]の中の田地2[[段 (単位)|段]]（720[[坪]]）が寄進され、[[明徳]]4年（[[1392年]]）には在地豪族である源満重から正月に1段、8月に修理料用として2段の田地が寄進され<ref>源満重は国衙もしくは守護の関係者と推測される（『島根県の地名』）。</ref>、嘉吉2年（[[1442年]]）には[[社領]]6[[町 (単位)|町]]1[[反]]余り（およそ22,000坪弱）が[[守護使不入]]の権利とともに[[安堵]]され、[[天正]]10年（[[1582年]]）には隠岐[[守護代]][[佐々木経清|佐々木（隠岐）経清]]が2[[名田|名内]]から4段半と280[[坪|歩]]の田地を寄進するなどしている<ref>以上、社家村尾家に伝わる「隠岐国在庁田地寄進状」等による。但し嘉吉2年の安堵状は、発給者を「源某」とするものの、[[花押]]が[[鎌倉時代]]の守護である[[佐々木泰清]]のものなので疑いがある（『島根県の地名』）。</ref>。殊に文明7年と明徳4年8月の寄進状には「公私御祈祷所」と記されているので、国衙からの信仰の対象とされていたこともわかり<ref name="平凡社">『島根県の地名』。</ref>、以上のことから当神社を中世における隠岐国[[一宮]]とする説もあるが（『[[隠岐国代考証]]』）、これは隠岐国ではなく[[郡]]（[[海部郡 (隠岐国)|海部郡]]）の一宮または[[惣社]]と見るのが妥当であろう<ref name="平凡社"/>。また、『増補隠州記』に「此宮は根元天竺ふ里うた（わ）うの子孫たりと云伝る」とあることから、中世以降[[両部神道]]の影響を受けていたようで<ref>『式内社調査報告』。但し「天竺ふりう王」の意味は不明。</ref>、[[応永]]5年（[[1398年]]）、同11年、同19年、同21年、[[永禄]]9年（[[1566年]]）、[[文禄]]5年（[[1596年]]）などの[[奥書]]を持つ[[大般若経]]284巻も残されている<ref name="平凡社"/>。
近世には社領10[[石 (単位)|石]]を有し（『増補隠州記』）、[[寛文]]7年（[[1667年]]）の『[[隠州視聴合紀]]』に「前に花表（[[鳥居]]）を立て瑞籬（[[玉垣|瑞垣]]）長し」と当時の様子が記されている。
[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）10月郷社に列し、[[戦後]]は[[神社本庁]]に参加している。
== 神事 == [[天保]]4年（[[1833年]]）の『[[隠州風土記]]』によると、[[例祭]]は6月11日で、他に'''御座替神事'''（9月29日）、'''御座入神事'''（10月29日）等の記載があるが、[[グレゴリオ暦|太陽暦]]施行後は例祭日を[[7月11日]]に改めている。また、御座替、御座入の両神事は断絶し、その記録も残されていないというが<ref name="白水社">『日本の神々』。</ref>、御座替神事は島根県[[松江市]]の[[佐太神社]]のものが著名で、同神社では現在も[[神座]]の[[茣蓙]]を敷き替える神事として行われている<ref>[[享保]]2年（[[1717年]]）の『[[雲陽誌]]』によれば、近世までは[[出雲国|出雲]]の複数の神社で行われていたようである。</ref>。御座入神事は、現在も[[島後]]の神社では行われており、それは旧10月のいわゆる[[神在祭]]に参加して還御した神々に暖かく休んでもらうために、社殿の側面を茣蓙で覆うものであるという。この島後と出雲それぞれで行われるものと同じ名の神事が当神社で行われていたことは、両神事の意義を考察する上で貴重なものである<ref name="白水社"/>。
== 神職 == 村尾氏が[[社家]]として継承している。同氏がいつから勤めるようになったかは不明であるが、社殿造営などの[[棟札]]から少なくとも近世以降は継承されており、嘉吉2年の安堵状に「宇津賀左衛門尉」と見える宇津賀氏が、後に村尾を氏名としたものと見られている<ref>『式内社調査報告』。もっとも前掲注のようにこの嘉吉2年の安堵状には疑いがある。</ref>。
== 社殿 == [[画像:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|拝殿（中央）と本殿（左奥）]] [[本殿]]は大正6年（[[1917年]]）の造替にかかる桁行3間の[[銅板葺]]屋根の[[隠岐造]]で、海士町の[[有形文化財]]に指定されている。他に[[幣殿]]や[[拝殿]]などがある。
なお、現本殿の[[間|間数]]は2間半四方であるが、明治初年の『神社取調帳』では3間四方となっており、かつては隠岐国一宮である水若酢神社や惣社である[[玉若酢命神社]]の本殿と同規模、もしくはそれ以上の規模であった<ref>同取調帳によると、水若酢、玉若酢命の両神社とも間口2間奥行3間となっている。</ref>。
== 文化財 == 海士町指定（<small>件名に続く括弧内は指定年月日</small>） *本殿（有形文化財・建造物）（[[平成]]9年（[[1997年]]）7月10日） *大般若経434巻（有形文化財・書跡）（同上）
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58年 *[[谷川健一]]編『日本の神々－神社と聖地』第7巻山陰《新装復刊》、白水社、2000年ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9（初版は1985年ISBN 4-560-02217-8） *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）、平凡社、1995年ISBN 4-582-49033-6
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:うすかみことしんしや}} [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社|名うすかみことしんしや]] [[Category:島根県の旧郷社]] [[Category:海士町の建築物]] [[Category:海士町の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 宇受賀命神社 |画像 = [[画像:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = 島根県隠岐郡海士町宇受賀747 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|06|57.9|N|133|06|32.2|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 宇受賀命 |社格 = 式内社（名神大）・旧郷社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = 隠岐造銅板葺 |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 7月11日 |神事 = }}
'''宇受賀命神社'''（うづかみことじんじゃ）は、[[島根県]][[隠岐郡]][[海士町]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。[[式内社]]（[[名神大社]]）で[[旧社格]]は[[郷社]]。[[島前]]の[[中ノ島 (島根県)|中ノ島]]の北端にあたる宇受賀部落の外れ、丘上に鎮座する。
== 祭神 == '''宇受賀命'''（うずかのみこと）を祀る。「宇須賀宮」や「宇津賀大明神」などと称され、一貫してこの地の[[守護神]]として祀られて来た他地に見えない当神社独自の[[神 (神道)|神]]である。神名は鎮座地「宇受賀」とも関係するものであろうが、語義不明。なお、[[元禄]]16年（[[1703年]]）の『島前村々神名記』には、祭神を「[[倉稲魂命|倉稲魂命（うかのみたまのみこと）]]」と記してあるが、これは「[[記紀]]」に載せる神名に付会させたものであろう<ref name="調査報告">『式内社調査報告』。</ref>。
== 由緒 == [[貞享]]5年（[[1688年]]）の『[[増補隠州記]]』によれば、[[嘉吉|嘉吉年中]]（15世紀中頃）に炎上して[[縁起]]を焼失したとあり、創祀の年代や事由は不明であるが上述のように鎮座地宇受賀の守護神として古くから祀られて来た。[[大正]]頃（20世紀前葉）の社蔵記録によると、[[遷座]]の時期は詳らかにしないものの、かつては現在地から北方150[[間]]（およそ270[[メートル]]）隔たった、海面から36、7[[尺]]程（およそ10メートル強）の高さの断崖上に鎮座していたという<ref name="調査報告"/>。『[[続日本後紀]]』に[[承和 (日本)|承和]]9年（[[842年]]）由良比女命神（[[由良比女神社]]）、水若酢命神（[[水若酢神社]]）とともに[[官社]]に預かったことが見え<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9年9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref>、隠岐国ではもっとも早く中央の史書に現れる神社の1社で、[[延喜式神名帳|延喜の制]]では[[延喜式神名帳|国幣大社]]（名神大社）に列した隠岐国4大社の1社でもあり、[[国内神名帳]]である『[[隠岐国神名帳]]』にも「従一位宇酒賀大明神」と記載を見る<ref>『続後紀』や『延喜式』での記載は「宇受'''加'''命神（社）」。なお、『国内神名帳』は玉若酢命神社の[[社家|神主家]]である[[億岐氏|億岐家]]に伝わるものであるが、後世の手が加わっているらしいことから、そこに見える「従一位」の[[神階]]は、[[吉田家]]の[[宗源宣旨]]によるもの、あるいは億岐氏の作為が働いたものと見られる（『式内社調査報告』）。</ref>。
中世を通じて[[国衙]]や[[守護|守護職]]から重きを置かれ、[[文明 (日本)|文明]]7年（[[1271年]]）に当地の[[田荘]]と[[目代]]から祭祀料として[[国衙領]]の中の田地2[[段 (単位)|段]]（720[[坪]]）が寄進され、[[明徳]]4年（[[1392年]]）には在地豪族である源満重から正月に1段、8月に修理料用として2段の田地が寄進され<ref>源満重は国衙もしくは守護の関係者と推測される（『島根県の地名』）。</ref>、嘉吉2年（[[1442年]]）には[[社領]]6[[町 (単位)|町]]1[[反]]余り（およそ22,000坪弱）が[[守護使不入]]の権利とともに[[安堵]]され、[[天正]]10年（[[1582年]]）には隠岐[[守護代]][[佐々木経清|佐々木（隠岐）経清]]が2[[名田|名内]]から4段半と280[[坪|歩]]の田地を寄進するなどしている<ref>以上、社家村尾家に伝わる「隠岐国在庁田地寄進状」等による。但し嘉吉2年の安堵状は、発給者を「源某」とするものの、[[花押]]が[[鎌倉時代]]の守護である[[佐々木泰清]]のものなので疑いがある（『島根県の地名』）。</ref>。殊に文明7年と明徳4年8月の寄進状には「公私御祈祷所」と記されているので、国衙からの信仰の対象とされていたこともわかり<ref name="平凡社">『島根県の地名』。</ref>、以上のことから当神社を中世における隠岐国[[一宮]]とする説もあるが（『[[隠岐国代考証]]』）、これは隠岐国ではなく[[郡]]（[[海部郡 (隠岐国)|海部郡]]）の一宮または[[惣社]]と見るのが妥当であろう<ref name="平凡社"/>。また、『増補隠州記』に「此宮は根元天竺ふ里うた（わ）うの子孫たりと云伝る」とあることから、中世以降[[両部神道]]の影響を受けていたようで<ref>『式内社調査報告』。但し「天竺ふりう王」の意味は不明。</ref>、[[応永]]5年（[[1398年]]）、同11年、同19年、同21年、[[永禄]]9年（[[1566年]]）、[[文禄]]5年（[[1596年]]）などの[[奥書]]を持つ[[大般若経]]284巻も残されている<ref name="平凡社"/>。
近世には社領10[[石 (単位)|石]]を有し（『増補隠州記』）、[[寛文]]7年（[[1667年]]）の『[[隠州視聴合紀]]』に「前に花表（[[鳥居]]）を立て瑞籬（[[玉垣|瑞垣]]）長し」と当時の様子が記されている。
[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）10月郷社に列し、[[戦後]]は[[神社本庁]]に参加している。
== 神事 == [[天保]]4年（[[1833年]]）の『[[隠州風土記]]』によると、[[例祭]]は6月11日で、他に'''御座替神事'''（9月29日）、'''御座入神事'''（10月29日）等の記載があるが、[[グレゴリオ暦|太陽暦]]施行後は例祭日を[[7月11日]]に改めている。また、御座替、御座入の両神事は断絶し、その記録も残されていないというが<ref name="白水社">『日本の神々』。</ref>、御座替神事は島根県[[松江市]]の[[佐太神社]]のものが著名で、同神社では現在も[[神座]]の[[茣蓙]]を敷き替える神事として行われている<ref>[[享保]]2年（[[1717年]]）の『[[雲陽誌]]』によれば、近世までは[[出雲国|出雲]]の複数の神社で行われていたようである。</ref>。御座入神事は、現在も[[島後]]の神社では行われており、それは旧10月のいわゆる[[神在祭]]に参加して還御した神々に暖かく休んでもらうために、社殿の側面を茣蓙で覆うものであるという。この島後と出雲それぞれで行われるものと同じ名の神事が当神社で行われていたことは、両神事の意義を考察する上で貴重なものである<ref name="白水社"/>。
== 神職 == 村尾氏が[[社家]]として継承している。同氏がいつから勤めるようになったかは不明であるが、社殿造営などの[[棟札]]から少なくとも近世以降は継承されており、嘉吉2年の安堵状に「宇津賀左衛門尉」と見える宇津賀氏が、後に村尾を氏名としたものと見られている<ref>『式内社調査報告』。もっとも前掲注のようにこの嘉吉2年の安堵状には疑いがある。</ref>。
== 社殿 == [[画像:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|拝殿（中央）と本殿（左奥）]] [[本殿]]は大正6年（[[1917年]]）の造替にかかる桁行3間の[[銅板葺]]屋根の[[隠岐造]]で、海士町の[[有形文化財]]に指定されている。他に[[幣殿]]や[[拝殿]]などがある。
なお、現本殿の[[間|間数]]は2間半四方であるが、明治初年の『神社取調帳』では3間四方となっており、かつては隠岐国一宮である水若酢神社や惣社である[[玉若酢命神社]]の本殿と同規模、もしくはそれ以上の規模であった<ref>同取調帳によると、水若酢、玉若酢命の両神社とも間口2間奥行3間となっている。</ref>。
== 文化財 == 海士町指定（<small>件名に続く括弧内は指定年月日</small>） *本殿（有形文化財・建造物）（[[平成]]9年（[[1997年]]）7月10日） *大般若経434巻（有形文化財・書跡）（同上）
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58年 *[[谷川健一]]編『日本の神々－神社と聖地』第7巻山陰《新装復刊》、白水社、2000年ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9（初版は1985年ISBN 4-560-02217-8） *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）、平凡社、1995年ISBN 4-582-49033-6

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{ill|36°06′57.9″N 133°06′32.2″E|ja|}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6
{{Shinto Horizontal}}

{{Shrine | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{ill|36°06′57.9″N 133°06′32.2″E|ja|}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|ja|海士町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|ja|隠岐郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu era|ja|嘉吉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|ja|水若酢神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden|ja|幣殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and a {{ill|Haiden|ja|拝殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|ja|7月11日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449116]] Wikidata name: Uzukamikoto-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg | location = [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Oki District, Shimane|Oki District, Shimane]], [[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama, Shimane]] Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{Coord|36.116083|N|133.108944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = [[:en:July 11|July 11]] | prefecture = {{ill|Ama|qid=Q685865}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama, Shimane]], [[:en:Oki District, Shimane|Oki District, Shimane]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the [[:en:Kakitsu|Kakitsu era]] (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and [[:en:Mizuwakasu Shrine|Mizuwakasu Shrine]] as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|qq=|1=Kokuhei Taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|qq=|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a [[:en:Heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] and a [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]].
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on [[:en:July 11|July 11]]. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6
{{Shinto Horizontal}}

[[Category:Completion tier 5]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449116]] Wikidata name: Uzukamikoto-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg | location = [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Oki District, Shimane|Oki District, Shimane]], [[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama, Shimane]] Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{Coord|36.116083|N|133.108944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = [[:en:July 11|July 11]] | prefecture = {{ill|Ama|qid=Q685865}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama, Shimane]], [[:en:Oki District, Shimane|Oki District, Shimane]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the [[:en:Kakitsu|Kakitsu era]] (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and [[:en:Mizuwakasu Shrine|Mizuwakasu Shrine]] as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|qq=|1=Kokuhei Taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|qq=|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a [[:en:Heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] and a [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]].
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on [[:en:July 11|July 11]]. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a [[:en:Heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] and a [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]].
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on [[:en:July 11|July 11]]. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6
