<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uzukamikoto Jinja]][[ja:宇受賀命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uzukamikoto Jinja]][[ja:宇受賀命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Uzukamikoto Jinja]][[ja:宇受賀命神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449116]] Wikidata name: Uzukamikoto-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg | location = [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Oki District, Shimane|Oki District, Shimane]], [[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama, Shimane]] Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{Coord|36.116083|N|133.108944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = [[:en:July 11|July 11]] | prefecture = {{ill|Ama|WD=Q685865}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ama, Shimane|Ama, Shimane]], [[:en:Oki District, Shimane|Oki District, Shimane]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the [[:en:Kakitsu|Kakitsu era]] (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and [[:en:Mizuwakasu Shrine|Mizuwakasu Shrine]] as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|1=Kokuhei Taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]], one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a [[:en:Heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] and a [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]].
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on [[:en:July 11|July 11]]. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.

== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a [[:en:Heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] and a [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]].
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on [[:en:July 11|July 11]]. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uzukamikoto Jinja]][[ja:宇受賀命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uzukamikoto Jinja]][[ja:宇受賀命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Uzukamikoto Jinja]][[ja:宇受賀命神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449116]] Wikidata name: Uzukamikoto-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{Coord|36.116083|N|133.108944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11}} | prefecture = {{ill|Ama|WD=Q685865}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu era}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|en|Mizuwakasu Shrine|WD=Q11549109|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|1=Kokuhei Taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden (Shinto)|en|Heiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3076699|lt=Heiden}} and a {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu era}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|en|Mizuwakasu Shrine|WD=Q11549109|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|1=Kokuhei Taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden (Shinto)|en|Heiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3076699|lt=Heiden}} and a {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{Coord|36.116083|N|133.108944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11}} | prefecture = {{ill|Ama|WD=Q685865}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu era}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|en|Mizuwakasu Shrine|WD=Q11549109|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|1=Kokuhei Taisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden (Shinto)|en|Heiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3076699|lt=Heiden}} and a {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden (Shinto)|en|Heiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3076699|lt=Heiden|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and a {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11|1=July 11}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane|1=Ama, Shimane}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{Coord|36.116083|N|133.108944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11|1=July 11}} | prefecture = {{ill|Ama|WD=Q685865|1=Ama, Shimane}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane|1=Ama, Shimane}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu era|1=Kakitsu}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|en|Mizuwakasu Shrine|WD=Q11549109|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|1=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|1=Q9685417|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden (Shinto)|en|Heiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3076699|lt=Heiden|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and a {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11|1=July 11}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.

'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane|1=Ama, Shimane}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu era|1=Kakitsu}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|en|Mizuwakasu Shrine|WD=Q11549109|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|1=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|1=Q9685417|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden (Shinto)|en|Heiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3076699|lt=Heiden|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and a {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11|1=July 11}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|1=Q9685417|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden (Shinto)|en|Heiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3076699|lt=Heiden|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and a {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11|1=July 11}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == *式内社研究會編『式内社調査報告 第21巻 山陰道4』 皇學館大學出版部、昭和58th year *[[Ken'ichi Tanigawa|Ken’ichi Tanigawa]] ed.『日本の神々－神社と聖地』Volume 7 (San'in Region), Hakusuisha, 2000, ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 *『島根県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系33）, Heibonsha, 1995, ISBN 4-582-49033-6

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Uzuka Mikoto Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja2.jpg | location = {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane|1=Ama, Shimane}} Uzuka 747 | coordinates = {{Coord|36.116083|N|133.108944|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Uzuka no Mikoto | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (under {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}), former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = Unknown | Main hall = Oki-style, copper roofing | Alternate name = | Annual festival = {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11|1=July 11}} | prefecture = {{ill|Ama|WD=Q685865|1=Ama, Shimane}} }}
'''Uzuka Mikoto Shrine''' (宇受賀命神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ama, Shimane|en|Ama, Shimane|WD=Q685865|lt=Ama, Shimane|1=Ama, Shimane}}, {{ill|Oki District, Shimane|en|Oki District, Shimane|WD=Q735735|lt=Oki District, Shimane|1=Oki District, Shimane}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, Japan. It is designated as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, specifically a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, and was formerly classified as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the modern Ranking system. The shrine is situated on a hill on the northern edge of Nakajima (part of the Oki Islands), in the Uzuka settlement.
== deity == The shrine is dedicated to '''Uzuka no Mikoto''' (宇受賀命), who has been worshipped as the local guardian deity of Uzuka. Known also as "Usuka-no-miya" and "Utsuka Daimyōjin," this unique deity is not found in other locations. The name appears to be related to the area’s name, "Uzuka," but its etymology is unclear. According to a 1703 document, the deity was linked to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, though this attribution may have been an attempt to align it with deities mentioned in classical texts.<ref name="Investigation Report">『式内社調査報告』</ref>
== History == According to the {{ill|Zōho Onshūki|ja|増補隠州記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōho Onshūki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1688), the shrine burned down during the {{ill|Kakitsu|en|Kakitsu|WD=Q1193238|lt=Kakitsu era|1=Kakitsu}} (15th century), destroying its origins and founding documents. However, it has long served as the guardian shrine for the Uzuka area. Records from the early Taishō period indicate that the shrine was previously located on a cliff approximately 270 meters north of its current site.<ref name="Investigation Report" />
The shrine first appears in the historical record in {{ill|Shōwa 9|ja|承和9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa 9|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (842), mentioned alongside {{ill|Yurahime Shrine|ja|由良比女神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yurahime Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Mizuwakasu Shrine|en|Mizuwakasu Shrine|WD=Q11549109|lt=Mizuwakasu Shrine|1=Mizuwakasu Shrine}} as receiving state support.<ref>『続日本後紀』承和9th year 9月乙巳（14日）条。</ref> It is one of the earliest Oki shrines mentioned in central records. The shrine was listed as a {{ill|Kokuhei Taisha|ja|国幣大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhei Taisha|1=Q9685417|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9685417}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}, one of four major shrines in Oki Province.
Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was supported by the provincial government and local rulers. For example, in 1271 (Bunmei 7), shrine lands were donated by officials, and additional lands were added by the local lord Minamoto no Mitsushige in the 14th century.<ref>『島根県の地名』</ref> During the late Kamakura period, the shrine held 22,000 tsubo (approximately 7 hectares) of protected lands.<ref name="Heibonsha">『島根県の地名』</ref>
In the Edo period, the shrine was recorded as having 10 koku of land and was described in detail in the {{ill|Onshū Shichō Gōki|ja|隠州視聴合紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onshū Shichō Gōki|1=Inshū Shichō Gōki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659057}} (1667).
In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated a Gōsha. It joined the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|WD=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} after World War II.
== Shrine Buildings == [[File:Uduka-mikoto-jinja1.jpg|thumb|170px|Haiden (center) and Honden (left rear)]] The main hall (Honden), rebuilt in 1917 (Taishō 6), is a three-bay structure in the Oki architectural style with copper roofing. It is designated as a Tangible Cultural Property of Ama Town. Additional structures include a {{ill|Heiden (Shinto)|en|Heiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3076699|lt=Heiden|1=Heiden (Shinto)}} and a {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}.
== Festivals == The main annual festival is held on {{ill|July 11|en|July 11|WD=Q2701|lt=July 11|1=July 11}}. Historical records also mention rituals such as the "Gozagae Shinji" (September 29) and "Gozairi Shinji" (October 29), but these have been discontinued.<ref name="Hakusuisha">『日本の神々』</ref> These rituals were significant for transitioning sacred seats and were once part of regional traditions.
== Cultural Properties == === Ama Town Designations === * **Honden** (Tangible Cultural Property, Architecture) - Designated July 10, 1997. * **Daihannya Kyō (Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra), 434 volumes** (Tangible Cultural Property, Writings) - Designated July 10, 1997.
